project.title: Comparison of human primordial oocyte follicles to ovarian somatic cells
project.projectDescription: Analysis primordial follicle compared to ovarian somatic cells proteome in humans.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Human primordial follicles and ovarian somatic cells were collected from two individuals who underwent ovarian surgery. Samples were dissolved in 6M Guanidine hydrochloride (GuaCl) pH 8.5, diluted to 2M GuaCl and digested with LysC (10 ng/µl) overnight. Samples were further diluted down to 0.5M GuaCl and digested with LysC (10 ng/µl) and Trypsin (5 ng/µl) for 8 hours at 37ºC. Samples were acidified by 5% formic acid and de-salted with home-made C18 columns. Samples were acquired in data-dependent adquisition (DDA) and full MS were detected in the Orbitrap. In each cycle of data-dependent acquisition analysis, the most intense ions were selected for fragmentation. Fragment ion spectra were produced via high-energy collision dissociation (HCD) at normalized collision energy of 28% and they were acquired in the ion trap mass analyzer.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: Acquired spectra were analyzed using the Proteome Discoverer software suite (v2.3, Thermo Fisher Scientific) and the Mascot search engine (v2.6, Matrix Science). Data were searched against SwissProt Human database. False discovery rate (FDR) in peptide identification was set to a maximum of 5%. Peptide quantification were retrieved from the “Precursor ion area detector” node. The obtained values were used to calculate an estimation of protein amount with the top3 area. Samples were normalized to COX4I1.

Comment[Instrument]: Orbitrap Eclipse mass spectrometer (Thermo) coupled to an EASY-nLC 1200 (Thermo)
Comment[AcquisitionMethod]: Data acquisition for TMT samples was performed using a Real Time Search MS3 method.
Comment[FragmentationMethod]: collision-induced dissociation at a normalized collision energy of 35%
Comment[Separation]: Peptides were separated on a 50-cm C18 column (Thermo)
Comment[GradientTime]: with a gradient from 4% to 32% acetonitrile in 90 min.
